scenario: FEEL: items list of 10 elements. Want items 3-5 (positions 3, 4, 5). Team wonders sublist function. FEEL sublist function?
question: FEEL sublist function?

options.text: md:281-317: - start position: 1-based; last position is -1 (negative indices count from end) per :291, :310. - length: optional; if omitted, takes from start to end (2-arg signature :281-298). - Examples (doc verbatim): - sublist([1,2,3], 2) → [2,3] per :296. - sublist([1,2,3], 1, 2) → [1,2] per :315. ) is the only documented slice form. md:88-113), not positional ranges.

FEEL does not support extracting sublists from larger list values -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. FEEL provides the sublist(list, start, length) function for extracting a portion of a list. List positions in FEEL are 1-based, meaning the first element is at position 1. FEEL also supports negative indices where -1 refers to the last element. Combined with functions like append, concatenate, and distinct values, FEEL offers comprehensive list manipulation capabilities.

FEEL list indexing is 0-based, with the first element at position 0 like most programming languages -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. FEEL uses 1-based list indexing where the first element is at position 1. This is consistent with DMN's business-oriented heritage where counting from 1 is more natural. FEEL also supports negative indices from -1 for the last element. The sublist function uses these 1-based positions: sublist([1,2,3,4], 2, 2) returns [2,3].

The split function in FEEL is designed for splitting strings by a delimiter, not for extracting sublists from list values. The appropriate function for list slicing is sublist(list, start position, length). Using split on a list value would produce an error. Understanding the distinction between string splitting and list slicing is important for writing correct FEEL expressions.

a.text: Correct. FEEL sublist: Signature: sublist(list, start [, length]) Examples: `feel sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 1, 3) → [1, 2, 3] sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 2, 3) → [2, 3, 4] sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 3) → [3, 4, 5] (no length) sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 3, 0) → [] (zero length) Verify exact signature per FEEL version. For 1-based (per Set 16 Q24): Position 1: first element. Position 3: third element. For the scenario (positions 3-5): feel sublist(items, 3, 3) Items at positions 3, 4, 5 (3 elements). Range-indexing syntax items[3..5] is NOT documented in C8 FEEL -- sublist(...) is the only documented slice form per feel-built-in-functions-list.md:281-317. Square-bracket list[c] syntax is reserved for filtering with a boolean predicate c (per feel-context-expressions.md:88-113). For out-of-bounds: feel sublist([1, 2, 3], 5, 2) → [] or error sublist([1, 2, 3], 2, 10) → [2, 3] (clamp at end) Verify behavior per version. For negative indices (documented in C8 per feel-built-in-functions-list.md:291, :310 verbatim "The last position is -1"): feel sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], -2) → [4, 5] (last 2; start at position -2 = element 4) sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], -1) → [5] (last 1) From end. Confirmed supported in C8 FEEL. Common use cases: Use case 1: Pagination: feel pageItems: sublist(items, (page - 1) * pageSize + 1, pageSize) Page-based slicing. Use case 2: Take first N: feel topN: sublist(sortedItems, 1, N) (No alternative range-bracket form in C8 FEEL -- sublist is the only way.) Use case 3: Skip first N: feel rest: sublist(items, N + 1) Skip first N. Use case 4: Middle portion: feel middle: sublist(items, 4, 5) Items 4-8. For combining with sort: feel sortedTop: sublist(sort(items, function(a, b) a.priority > b.priority), 1, 5) Top 5 by priority. For dynamic slicing: feel recentN: sublist(items, count(items) - N + 1, N) Last N items. Range-indexing syntax is NOT in C8 FEEL: list[startPos..endPos] syntax does not exist in Camunda 8 FEEL (Grep across components/modeler/feel/ returned no documentation). Square-bracket list[c] is filter syntax -- c must be a boolean predicate per feel-context-expressions.md:88-113, NOT a numeric range. For positional slicing, sublist(list, start, length) is the only documented form. For first / last N: feel // first N sublist(items, 1, N) // last N (negative start position) sublist(items, -N) For empty list: feel sublist([], 1, 5) → [] Safe. For null list: feel sublist(null, 1, 5) → error or null (per version) Defensive: check before. For combining with other operations: feel // Top 5 unique distinct values(sublist(sort(items), 1, 5)) Chain operations. For DMN tables: In output expressions: slice lists. In input entries: less common. For testing: Test boundary cases: start=1, end=count. Out of bounds: behavior. Best practices: Use sublist for clear slicing. Use range indexing for compact. Test edge cases. Anti-patterns: Manual loops for slicing: when built-in fits. No bounds check: errors. For the scenario (positions 3-5): sublist(items, 3, 3)` -- only documented C8 FEEL form.

explanation: FEEL sublist: Signature: sublist(list, start [, length]) Examples: `feel sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 1, 3) → [1, 2, 3] sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 2, 3) → [2, 3, 4] sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 3) → [3, 4, 5] (no length) sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 3, 0) → [] (zero length) Verify exact signature per FEEL version. For 1-based (per Set 16 Q24): Position 1: first element. Position 3: third element. For the scenario (positions 3-5): feel sublist(items, 3, 3) Items at positions 3, 4, 5 (3 elements). Range-indexing syntax items[3..5] is NOT documented in C8 FEEL -- sublist(...) is the only documented slice form per feel-built-in-functions-list.md:281-317. Square-bracket list[c] syntax is reserved for filtering with a boolean predicate c (per feel-context-expressions.md:88-113). For out-of-bounds: feel sublist([1, 2, 3], 5, 2) → [] or error sublist([1, 2, 3], 2, 10) → [2, 3] (clamp at end) Verify behavior per version. For negative indices (documented in C8 per feel-built-in-functions-list.md:291, :310 verbatim "The last position is -1"): feel sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], -2) → [4, 5] (last 2; start at position -2 = element 4) sublist([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], -1) → [5] (last 1) From end. Confirmed supported in C8 FEEL. Common use cases: Use case 1: Pagination: feel pageItems: sublist(items, (page - 1) * pageSize + 1, pageSize) Page-based slicing. Use case 2: Take first N: feel topN: sublist(sortedItems, 1, N) (No alternative range-bracket form in C8 FEEL -- sublist is the only way.) Use case 3: Skip first N: feel rest: sublist(items, N + 1) Skip first N. Use case 4: Middle portion: feel middle: sublist(items, 4, 5) Items 4-8. For combining with sort: feel sortedTop: sublist(sort(items, function(a, b) a.priority > b.priority), 1, 5) Top 5 by priority. For dynamic slicing: feel recentN: sublist(items, count(items) - N + 1, N) Last N items. Range-indexing syntax is NOT in C8 FEEL: list[startPos..endPos] syntax does not exist in Camunda 8 FEEL (Grep across components/modeler/feel/ returned no documentation). Square-bracket list[c] is filter syntax -- c must be a boolean predicate per feel-context-expressions.md:88-113, NOT a numeric range. For positional slicing, sublist(list, start, length) is the only documented form. For first / last N: feel // first N sublist(items, 1, N) // last N (negative start position) sublist(items, -N) For empty list: feel sublist([], 1, 5) → [] Safe. For null list: feel sublist(null, 1, 5) → error or null (per version) Defensive: check before. For combining with other operations: feel // Top 5 unique distinct values(sublist(sort(items), 1, 5)) Chain operations. For DMN tables: In output expressions: slice lists. In input entries: less common. For testing: Test boundary cases: start=1, end=count. Out of bounds: behavior. Best practices: Use sublist for clear slicing. Use range indexing for compact. Test edge cases. Anti-patterns: Manual loops for slicing: when built-in fits. No bounds check: errors. For the scenario (positions 3-5): sublist(items, 3, 3)` -- only documented C8 FEEL form.
